author: @geeforce3891
text:
awesome and amazing when you think about it! I guess the real question here is how viable this option for producing renewable energy is. I suppose it depends of efficiency etc and I wonder how much available potential power can come from this?

I've just uploaded 3 vids on my channel, a 4th on the way! on super caps and carbon foam based on the work you've showed us. I hope you enjoy and find it interesting! Tim

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i'm going to go watch mate - awesome - cheers

author: @zeb1820
text:
Awesome, quick question if you don't mind, and apologies if I missed the answer somewhere already: is the energy primarily from the plant, via the 'protein exchange' clay, or is it not just the carbon and foil that is generating the charge, as per your previous battery designs? Any info on how the energy is transferred from the plant would be appreciated.

I'm a biochemist and microbiologist and have experience in protein and microbe based fuel cells, but this plant cell is new to me, so am fascinated. Love the way you share your research and understanding with us, thank you! A true example of a 'good scientist'.

Also, is the live event online, or which physical location? Would be keen to join.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's from the redox reaction of the soil bacteria which 'feed' on the plant waste that results from photosynthesis

author: @Buzzhumma
text: So great. Maybe red led so the audience can get a visual but 5 cells in series to up the voltage. Maybe a few led   could go in the kit too as a suggestion as they are cheap enough

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate - there is much more to it than that - you need to read the research - the induction motor works well as it will continue to spin down to 0.08v whereas a LED needs quite a high forward voltage even though the mA draw is low.

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I meant 5 moss cells in series and 1 led. Would that work? I assume they can go in series but maybe they can't, I shall read the paper soon. Very cool

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah mate it will do

replies.author: @energycrafts8206
replies.text: Robert is that right?  You said the motor will spin even down at 0.08v.  Less than 1/10 volt to run the motor seems unreal, wow:  was expecting more like 0.8v at best.  And at what amps?  I ran toy motors and LEDs from very modified crystal radio like circuits that work with very small powers scavenged from various RF sources via the earth ground and/or aerial wire "antenna" and such.  And yes, even made connections to trees and plants in my exploration.  Used capacitors to store the feeble intake until enough to power a useful load.  It seems feeble (diffuse) energy is everywhere:  plants, animals, soil, air and even arriving to earth from outer space besides the sun!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yes mate it is - these are awesome motors - I get them from toy quadcopters they are 8mm in diameter and 14mm long - or there abouts - and they run down to 0.08v and draw around 15-10mA - so they are great for low voltage but not so good at high amps - an LED will draw around 2mA to flicker on. so if used directly with very very low power sources the quickly run out - as seen in the first vid i did in this series - it's one of the reasons i put a cap in the circuit - cheers

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: as long as the moss continues to grow

author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
text: Well done, but 0.18 Volts is a bit low... Can you get higher voltages by longer charging the supercap ? What is the maximum open circuit voltage of the cell, if only a high ohmic digital voltmeter is connected ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 0.3 volts - and it's clearly not too low as it is sufficient to do work - but then it is what it is - a plant microbial fuel cell!

replies.author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Ahh I see, just  0.3 Volts, anyway, so you need to put a few cells in series to get a better voltage to get an LED to light up from the supercap...
 if you would take a Zink plate as the lower plate it would generate much more, but then it would be again more like a primary cell eating up the zink plate...Anyway, well done !

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would mate - but you don't really want to poison the system by introducing high concentrtions of free metal ions

replies.author: @StefanHartmann-hartiberlin
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes, but Zink metal ions are not toxic, but help the plants even better growing... lol...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are missing the point mate - we have two choices - force a system and kill it or work with it and keep it alive - i am not saying one is better or not - they are just choices - Zinc, incidentally, is not non-toxic - just search zinc environmental toxicity on google
